results.question: Is this patient allergic to penicillin?
results.expected: YES — urticaria and angioedema. Moderate. Documented since 1985. Avoid all penicillin derivatives.
results.answer:
Patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782) has a documented allergy to penicillin. The reaction is described as moderate, with urticaria and angioedema. This allergy has been documented since childhood, and the patient avoids all penicillin derivatives. 

Additional allergies include sulfonamides (mild rash), shellfish (severe anaphylaxis, carries EpiPen), and iodinated contrast (moderate flushing and bronchospasm, requires premedication) [S2][S3].

results.question: Can this patient receive iodinated contrast?
results.expected: NOT without premedication — flushing, nausea, mild bronchospasm (2021 cath). Premedicate with steroids and diphenhydramine.
results.answer:
Patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782) has a documented moderate allergy to iodinated contrast. His reactions include flushing and mild bronchospasm that occurred during a cardiac catheterization in 2021. Due to this allergy, iodinated contrast administration requires premedication with steroids and diphenhydramine to mitigate the risk of adverse reactions [S2][S3].

Therefore, he can receive iodinated contrast only with appropriate premedication and caution.

results.question: Does this patient carry an EpiPen?
results.expected: Yes — shellfish anaphylaxis (throat swelling, hypotension). Last reaction 2018, required ED visit.
results.answer: Patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782) carries an EpiPen due to a severe shellfish allergy with a history of anaphylaxis. This allergy and the presence of the EpiPen are documented in his allergy section, noting the last reaction was in 2018 requiring an emergency department visit [S2][S3].

results.question: List all drug allergies
results.expected: Penicillin (moderate), Sulfonamides (mild — maculopapular rash 2010), Iodinated contrast (moderate)
results.answer:
For patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the documented drug allergies are:

- Penicillin: Moderate reaction with urticaria and angioedema; documented since childhood; avoids all penicillin derivatives.
- Sulfonamides: Mild reaction with maculopapular rash; developed rash with TMP-SMX in 2010.
- Iodinated contrast: Moderate reaction with flushing, nausea, and mild bronchospasm; occurred during cardiac catheterization in 2021; requires premedication with steroids and diphenhydramine.

Additionally, he has a severe allergy to shellfish causing anaphylaxis (throat swelling, hypotension), for which he carries an EpiPen.

These allergies are well documented with severity and reaction details [S2][S3].

results.question: What is the patient's code status?
results.expected: Full Code. Healthcare POA: Linda Whitfield (wife). Advance directive on file.
results.answer: For patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the documented code status is Full Code. The patient also has an advance directive and a healthcare power of attorney designated as Linda Whitfield (wife) [Source 2].

results.question: When should the patient call 911 or go to the ED?
results.expected: Chest pain, worsening SOB, weight gain >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week, syncope, palpitations >15 min, blood sugar >300 or <70 with symptoms, new leg swelling.
results.answer:
For patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the instructions for when to call 911 or go to the emergency department (ED) are as follows:

He should go to the ED or call 911 if he experiences any of these warning signs:
- Chest pain or pressure
- Increasing shortness of breath or worsening breathing difficulty
- Sudden weight gain of more than 2 pounds overnight
- Fainting or feeling like he might faint
- Heart racing (palpitations) lasting more than 15 minutes
- Blood sugar levels over 300 or under 70 accompanied by shakiness or sweating
- New leg swelling

These instructions are emphasized in his discharge and important reminders sections, along with daily weight monitoring and other self-care measures [S2][S4][S7].

results.question: What is the patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score?
results.expected: 4 — documented in Day 2 SOAP note and discharge prescriptions. Indication for apixaban anticoagulation.
results.answer: For patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the documented CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4, as noted in the plan section where anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg BID is planned based on this score [S2].

results.question: Does this patient have an ICD?
results.expected: No. ICD evaluation planned for April 2026 — 3 months post-GDMT optimization per ACC/AHA guidelines.
results.answer: Patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782) does not currently have an implanted ICD (implantable cardioverter defibrillator). The clinical notes indicate that an ICD evaluation is planned for approximately 3 months after guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) optimization, per current ACC/AHA guidelines. This evaluation is scheduled for April 2026, about 3 months post-discharge. The patient is undergoing optimization of heart failure therapy and will be reassessed for ICD candidacy at that time. There is no documentation of an existing ICD implant in the records reviewed [S1][S2][S3][S4][S6][S8].

results.question: What dietary restrictions apply at discharge?
results.expected: Sodium <2g/day, fluid restriction 1.5L/day, diabetic meal plan, heart-healthy diet. Avoid processed foods, canned soups, fast food.
results.answer:
For Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the dietary restrictions at discharge include:

- A strict low-sodium diet with sodium intake less than 2 grams per day.
- Fluid restriction limited to 1.5 liters per day.
- The diet should also be heart-healthy and diabetic-appropriate.

These instructions are part of his discharge plan to manage his heart failure and diabetes effectively. He was extensively educated on these restrictions before discharge [S2][S4][S5][S6].

results.question: What medications was the patient on at home?
results.expected: Lisinopril 20mg, Carvedilol 12.5mg BID, Furosemide 40mg, Metformin 1000mg BID, Atorvastatin 40mg, Aspirin 81mg, Tiotropium 2.5mcg, Omeprazole 20mg, Allopurinol 100mg, KCl 20mEq
results.answer:
For Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the home (admission) medications documented are:

- Lisinopril 20 mg once daily orally (ACE inhibitor for hypertension/CHF)
- Carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily orally (beta-blocker for CHF)
- Furosemide 40 mg once daily orally (loop diuretic for CHF)
- Metformin 1000 mg twice daily orally (for type 2 diabetes)
- Atorvastatin 40 mg once daily at bedtime orally (statin for hyperlipidemia/ASCVD prevention)
- Aspirin 81 mg once daily orally (antiplatelet post-PCI)
- Tiotropium bromide inhalation spray 2 puffs once daily (COPD maintenance)
- Omeprazole 20 mg once daily orally before breakfast (for stomach acid)

These represent the patient's active medications prior to admission as per the medication list and history sections [S1][S3].

results.question: What new medications were started during hospitalization?
results.expected: Sacubitril/Valsartan (ARNI), Spironolactone 25mg, Apixaban 5mg BID, Insulin Glargine, Empagliflozin 10mg, Metolazone, IV Furosemide, Heparin drip, Magnesium Oxide 400mg
results.answer:
During hospitalization for acute on chronic systolic heart failure, Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782) was started on several new medications as part of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) optimization and management of his conditions. The new medications initiated during the hospital stay include:

- Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto) 24/26 mg twice daily, replacing lisinopril after a 36-hour washout period.
- Spironolactone 25 mg once daily.
- Empagliflozin (Jardiance) 10 mg once daily in the morning for heart failure and type 2 diabetes benefit.
- Apixaban (Eliquis) 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation anticoagulation.
- Insulin Glargine (Lantus) started and titrated up to 18 units subcutaneously at bedtime for improved glycemic control.
- Magnesium oxide 400 mg once daily to prevent irregular heartbeat.
- Potassium chloride 20 mEq once daily to replace potassium lost from diuretics.
- Hydralazine 25 mg three times daily as needed for systolic blood pressure >160 mmHg.

These additions were alongside uptitration of carvedilol to 25 mg twice daily and increased furosemide dosing from home 40 mg to 80 mg daily orally. The patient was also continued on metformin, atorvastatin, aspirin, tiotropium, omeprazole, and allopurinol, which were part of his home regimen.

In summary, the new medications started during hospitalization were sacubitril/valsartan, spironolactone, empagliflozin, apixaban, insulin glargine, magnesium oxide, potassium chloride, and hydralazine (as needed) [S18b, S19, S21].

results.question: What are the discharge medications?
results.expected: 15 total: Entresto 24/26mg BID (new), Carvedilol 25mg BID (uptitrated), Furosemide 80mg (increased from 40mg), Spironolactone 25mg (new), Empagliflozin 10mg (new), Apixaban 5mg BID (new), Metformin 1000mg BID, Insulin Glargine 18u (new), Atorvastatin 40mg, Aspirin 81mg, Tiotropium, Omeprazole 20mg, Allopurinol 100mg, KCl 20mEq, Magnesium Oxide 400mg (new), Hydralazine 25mg PRN SBP>160
results.answer:
For Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), the discharge medication regimen includes the following 15 medications:

1. Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto) 24-26 mg, 1 tablet orally twice daily with food (replacing lisinopril; allow 36-hour washout from ACE inhibitor)
2. Carvedilol 25 mg, 1 tablet orally twice daily with food (uptitrated during hospitalization)
3. Furosemide 80 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily in the morning (increased from home dose)
4. Spironolactone 25 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily
5. Empagliflozin (Jardiance) 10 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily in the morning (new for HFrEF and type 2 diabetes benefit)
6. Apixaban (Eliquis) 5 mg, 1 tablet orally twice daily (new for atrial fibrillation anticoagulation)
7. Metformin 1000 mg, 1 tablet orally twice daily with meals
8. Insulin Glargine (Lantus) 18 units, subcutaneous injection once daily at bedtime (new for improved glycemic control)
9. Atorvastatin 40 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily at bedtime
10. Aspirin 81 mg EC, 1 tablet orally once daily
11. Tiotropium (Spiriva Respimat) 2.5 mcg/actuation, 2 puffs inhaled once daily
12. Omeprazole 20 mg, 1 capsule orally once daily before breakfast
13. Allopurinol 100 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily (for gout prophylaxis)
14. Potassium Chloride 20 mEq ER, 1 tablet orally once daily (to replace potassium lost from diuretics; monitor potassium levels)
15. Magnesium Oxide 400 mg, 1 tablet orally once daily (new to prevent irregular heartbeat)
16. Hydralazine 25 mg, 1 tablet orally three times daily as needed for systolic blood pressure >160 mmHg

This comprehensive regimen reflects optimization of guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, atrial fibrillation anticoagulation, diabetes management, COPD maintenance, and gastroprotection. The patient was discharged stable with home health services and detailed follow-up plans [S19][S22][S18].

results.question: Why was lisinopril stopped at discharge?
results.expected: Replaced by Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto). Require 36-hour washout from ACE inhibitor before starting ARNI. Lisinopril was also held on admission due to AKI.
results.answer:
For patient Robert James Whitfield (MRN: MRN-2026-004782), lisinopril was stopped at discharge to allow a 36-hour washout period before starting sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto). This is a standard precaution to avoid angioedema and other adverse effects when switching from an ACE inhibitor like lisinopril to an ARNI such as sacubitril/valsartan. The discharge prescriptions include sacubitril/valsartan 24-26 mg twice daily replacing lisinopril, with the explicit note to allow this washout period [S2][S3].

Additionally, the admission orders noted holding the home ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) per nephrology until creatinine improved, indicating renal function concerns may have also contributed to stopping lisinopril during hospitalization [S1].
